front: Skorokhod embedding for one mean-zero variable (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: If X has mean 0 and finite second moment, then Brownian motion can be stopped at a time T with B_T distributed as X and E T = E X^2. Exam use: Turns random-walk increments into Brownian increments with matching variance, making Brownian path results available for sums. Pitfall: The random interval used in the construction is external randomness; conditioning is what preserves the stopping-time calculations.

front: Embedding iid sums in Brownian motion (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: For iid mean-zero variance-one increments, one can choose increasing stopping times T_n so that S_n has the same finite-dimensional law as B(T_n), with iid gaps T_n-T_{n-1}. Exam use: Useful for proving CLT and invariance principles by comparing S_n to Brownian motion at random times. Pitfall: The equality is in distribution for the embedded walk, not necessarily pathwise equality with a preexisting walk.

front: Central limit theorem via random times (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: Since S_n/sqrt(n) has the same law as B(T_n)/sqrt(n) and T_n/n converges in probability to 1, S_n/sqrt(n) converges to a standard normal variable. Exam use: A clean proof strategy when Brownian embedding is available and finite variance is assumed. Pitfall: One still needs tight control of B(t) near t=1; T_n/n -> 1 alone is not a distributional identity.

front: Donsker invariance principle (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: The linearly interpolated process S(n t)/sqrt(n), built from iid mean-zero variance-one increments, converges weakly in C[0,1] to standard Brownian motion. Exam use: This is the main transfer principle from random walks to Brownian motion for maxima, hitting times, occupations, and path functionals. Pitfall: The topology matters: the statement is process-level weak convergence, not only convergence of each fixed time.

front: Continuous functional transfer from Donsker (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: If a functional on C[0,1] is continuous at Brownian paths with probability one, then applying it to the rescaled random walk converges to applying it to Brownian motion. Exam use: The default method for deriving asymptotics of path statistics from random-walk convergence. Pitfall: Many natural functionals are not everywhere continuous; check Brownian almost-sure continuity rather than ordinary continuity only.

front: Random-walk maximum limit (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: The normalized maximum max_{m<=n} S_m/sqrt(n) converges to max_{0<=t<=1} B_t, whose tail is 2 P(B_1 >= a). Exam use: Recognize maximum and boundary-crossing limits as Brownian supremum problems. Pitfall: The reflection-principle tail formula is for a nonnegative barrier and Brownian motion starting at 0.

front: Last zero before time n (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: The last sign-change or zero time of a centered finite-variance walk, divided by n, converges to the Brownian last-zero time, which has the arcsine law. Exam use: Transfers the arcsine law beyond simple symmetric walks. Pitfall: The functional is discontinuous at some deterministic paths, so the Brownian null-set condition is the key point.

front: Occupation time of a half-line (8.1 Donsker's Theorem)
back: The fraction of times a centered finite-variance walk spends above a sqrt(n) threshold converges to the Brownian occupation time above that threshold. Exam use: Use for random-walk occupation proportions and arcsine-type limits. Pitfall: Discrete-time counts require a small interpolation comparison; do not identify them blindly with continuous occupation time.

front: Embedding square-integrable martingales (8.2 CLTs for Martingales)
back: A square-integrable martingale can be represented in distribution as Brownian motion sampled at increasing stopping times whose conditional expected increments match conditional variances. Exam use: Provides the Brownian-time-change route to martingale CLTs. Pitfall: The conditional variance process, not deterministic time, controls the Brownian clock.

front: Martingale convergence from finite quadratic variation (8.2 CLTs for Martingales)
back: A square-integrable martingale with finite total conditional variance has an almost sure finite limit. Exam use: A standard way to separate negligible martingale tails from the main CLT-scale variance. Pitfall: Finite expected total variance is stronger than merely having bounded increments.

front: Martingale functional CLT by random clock (8.2 CLTs for Martingales)
back: For a martingale-difference array, if the embedded Brownian clock converges to deterministic time and large jumps vanish, the interpolated martingale converges to Brownian motion. Exam use: The process-level version of the martingale CLT; use when partial-sum paths rather than only final sums appear. Pitfall: The theorem needs both variance-clock convergence and a Lindeberg-type no-large-jumps condition.

front: Lindeberg-Feller condition for martingales (8.2 CLTs for Martingales)
back: A martingale-difference array has a normal limit when its conditional variances converge to 1 and its conditional large-jump contribution vanishes. Exam use: High-yield theorem for dependent CLTs where conditional means are zero. Pitfall: Ordinary variance convergence is not enough; the conditional variance process must stabilize.

front: Martingale central limit theorem (8.2 CLTs for Martingales)
back: A martingale-difference sequence normalized by its variance has a normal limit under conditional variance convergence and conditional Lindeberg conditions. Exam use: Use for sums of dependent variables after decomposing them into martingale differences. Pitfall: Verify the filtration and conditional mean-zero property before applying the theorem.

front: Stationary sequence CLT through martingale approximation (8.3 CLTs for Stationary Sequences)
back: For an ergodic stationary square-integrable sequence, a CLT can be proved when the partial sums are well approximated by a martingale with stationary differences. Exam use: Main strategy for dependent stationary sequences on prelim-style problems. Pitfall: Ergodicity and square integrability do not by themselves guarantee a CLT; the approximation condition matters.

front: Projective criterion for stationary CLT (8.3 CLTs for Stationary Sequences)
back: If conditional projections of future partial sums are summable or suitably controlled, stationary centered sequences satisfy a CLT with variance from the martingale approximation. Exam use: A practical check for dependent sequences where conditioning on the past becomes small. Pitfall: Do not replace the projective condition by ordinary covariance decay unless a theorem justifies it.

front: M-dependent stationary CLT (8.3 CLTs for Stationary Sequences)
back: For a centered stationary M-dependent sequence, the normalized sum has a normal limit with long-run variance sigma^2 = E X_0^2 + 2 sum_{k=1}^M E X_0 X_k. Exam use: Fast recognition pattern for finite-range dependence problems. Pitfall: The limiting variance can be zero; in that case the usual nondegenerate normal limit collapses.

front: Markov-chain CLT via Poisson equation (8.3 CLTs for Stationary Sequences)
back: For a stationary ergodic Markov chain, additive functionals can satisfy a CLT when f is represented through a solution of a Poisson equation, producing a martingale plus a telescoping error. Exam use: Use when a dependent sum is generated by a Markov transition kernel. Pitfall: Stationarity or a controlled initial distribution is needed; starting far from equilibrium can add transient terms.

front: Moving-average process CLT (8.3 CLTs for Stationary Sequences)
back: Linear moving averages of iid innovations satisfy CLTs under coefficient conditions that make the process square integrable and approximable by finite-dependent truncations. Exam use: A standard dependent but explicit model where long-run variance can often be computed. Pitfall: Absolute or square summability assumptions on coefficients are not cosmetic; they justify truncation and variance formulas.

front: Mixing covariance inequality (8.3.1 Mixing Properties)
back: Mixing coefficients bound covariances between variables measurable with respect to separated sigma-fields, with Holder exponents controlling integrability. Exam use: Converts abstract mixing assumptions into summable covariance or projection bounds for CLTs. Pitfall: Check the exact mixing coefficient and exponent relation; different texts use different normalizations.

front: Strong-mixing stationary CLT (8.3.1 Mixing Properties)
back: A centered ergodic stationary sequence satisfying suitable moment and summable mixing conditions obeys a CLT with long-run variance given by the covariance series. Exam use: Use for dependent sequences when decay of dependence is given quantitatively. Pitfall: Moment assumptions and summability rates are paired; weakening one often requires strengthening the other.

front: Glivenko-Cantelli theorem (8.4 Empirical Distributions, Brownian Bridge)
back: The empirical distribution function F_n converges uniformly almost surely to the true distribution function F. Exam use: The baseline consistency result for empirical CDFs before studying sqrt(n) fluctuations. Pitfall: Pointwise LLN is not enough; the theorem gives uniform convergence over all x.

front: Empirical process convergence to Brownian bridge (8.4 Empirical Distributions, Brownian Bridge)
back: For uniform samples, sqrt(n)(F_n(t)-t) converges as a process to the Brownian bridge B_t - t B_1. Exam use: Foundation for Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics and distribution-free empirical-process limits. Pitfall: The limit is a bridge, not free Brownian motion, because the empirical CDF is pinned at 0 and 1.

front: Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic limit (8.4 Empirical Distributions, Brownian Bridge)
back: The statistic D_n = sqrt(n) sup_x |F_n(x)-F(x)| converges to sup_{0<=t<=1} |B_t - t B_1| for continuous F. Exam use: Identifies the asymptotic null distribution of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness-of-fit statistic. Pitfall: Continuity of F gives distribution-free uniformization; atoms require separate handling.

front: Brownian bridge as pinned Brownian motion (8.4 Empirical Distributions, Brownian Bridge)
back: The Brownian bridge has the same law as B_t - t B_1 on [0,1], equivalently Brownian motion conditioned to return to 0 at time 1. Exam use: Use to compute bridge covariance, Markov transition laws, and empirical-process limits. Pitfall: Conditioning on an event of probability zero is shorthand for a regular conditional or limiting density argument.

front: Kolmogorov distribution by killed Brownian motion (8.4 Empirical Distributions, Brownian Bridge)
back: The distribution of sup |Brownian bridge| can be computed from Brownian motion killed on exiting an interval and then pinned at time 1. Exam use: Useful when an exam asks not just for the KS limit but for its explicit distribution. Pitfall: Keep straight whether the boundary event is one-sided or two-sided; the formulas differ.

front: Law of the iterated logarithm for Brownian motion (8.5 Laws of the Iterated Logarithm)
back: Brownian motion satisfies limsup_{t->infty} B_t / sqrt(2 t log log t) = 1 almost surely, with the corresponding liminf equal to -1. Exam use: The Brownian template for LILs of partial sums after embedding or invariance principles. Pitfall: The log log term is meaningful only in the eventual range where it is positive; constants are sharp.

front: Law of the iterated logarithm for iid sums (8.5 Laws of the Iterated Logarithm)
back: For iid mean-zero finite-variance increments with variance sigma^2, limsup S_n / sqrt(2 sigma^2 n log log n) = 1 almost surely, and the liminf is -1. Exam use: Use when a problem asks for sharp almost-sure fluctuation size of partial sums. Pitfall: A CLT-scale heuristic is too small for limsup behavior; LIL has an extra sqrt(log log n) factor.

front: Strassen invariance principle (8.5 Laws of the Iterated Logarithm)
back: The set of subsequential limit points of suitably LIL-rescaled partial-sum paths is the unit ball of absolutely continuous functions with square-integrable derivative. Exam use: A functional strengthening of the scalar LIL; it describes the whole path shape, not just limsup constants. Pitfall: The limit set is deterministic and compact in path space; it is not a single limiting process.
